posts.title: Shipped agents vs demo agents
posts.hook:
Every team I talk to is "building AI agents."

Maybe 10% have one running in production.
posts.body:
Every team I talk to is "building AI agents."

Maybe 10% have one running in production.

The gap isn't the model. It's not the framework. It's the boring stuff nobody wants to build.

Error recovery. Session persistence. Graceful degradation when your third-party API rate limits you at 2am. Logging that actually tells you why the agent chose path B over path A.

I've been running autonomous agents in production for months now. Daily crons, content pipelines, data enrichment loops. The agent code is maybe 20% of the system. The other 80% is:

✅ Health checks that catch silent failures
✅ Retry logic with exponential backoff
✅ State management so a crashed run picks up where it left off
✅ Cost tracking per execution so you don't wake up to a $400 bill

The teams shipping real agents aren't using fancier models. They're treating agents like production software instead of notebook experiments.

LangGraph, CrewAI, raw API calls. The framework matters way less than whether you built the scaffolding around it.

If your agent works in a demo but not at 3am on a Tuesday with bad data, you don't have an agent. You have a prototype.
posts.cta: What's the most annoying production agent bug you've hit? The silent failures are always the worst.

remixes.label: graveyard-of-demos
remixes.hook:
There's a graveyard of AI agent demos that never made it to production.

I've contributed to it. Multiple times.
remixes.body:
There's a graveyard of AI agent demos that never made it to production.

I've contributed to it. Multiple times.

The demo is the easy part. You wire up a model, give it tools, show it completing a task in a Loom video. Applause. Roadmap slot secured.

Then reality hits.

The agent works 80% of the time. The other 20% it silently does the wrong thing and nobody notices until a customer complains. You add logging. Now you're drowning in logs with no way to trace a decision chain. You add retries. Now you're handling idempotency across twelve API calls. You add state persistence. Now you're debugging serialization bugs at midnight.

The teams that actually ship agents treat them like backend services, not magic. They build observability first. They design for partial failure. They write the boring recovery code before they write the clever prompt.

I run agents 24/7 on a single Mac Mini. The ones that survive aren't the smartest. They're the ones with the best error handling, the cleanest state management, and the most predictable failure modes.

The model is maybe 20% of a production agent. The other 80% is plumbing.
remixes.cta: If your agent works in a notebook but not in prod, the fix probably isn't a better prompt. It's better infrastructure.

remixes.label: production-checklist
remixes.hook:
Before you ship an AI agent to production, run this checklist.

I learned every item the hard way.
remixes.body:
Before you ship an AI agent to production, run this checklist.

I learned every item the hard way.

1. Can it recover from a mid-run crash without restarting from zero? If not, your users will hate you when it happens at scale.

2. Do you know why it chose action A over action B? Not vibes. Actual logged reasoning you can audit.

3. What happens when an external API goes down? Not "it throws an error." What does the agent do next? Does it retry? Skip? Notify someone?

4. Is there a kill switch? Can you stop a runaway agent in under 30 seconds?

5. Can you replay a failed run with the exact same inputs? Reproducibility isn't optional when money or customer data is involved.

6. Do you have cost guardrails? An agent in a loop can burn through API credits faster than you can open your billing dashboard.

Most teams nail items 1 and 2 and skip the rest. Then they wonder why their agent "randomly breaks" in production.

It's not random. It's the failure modes you didn't design for.
remixes.cta: What would you add to this list? I keep a running version and update it every time something breaks.

Every team I talk to is "building AI agents."

Maybe 10% have one running in production.

And honestly? Most of that 10% is running glorified cron jobs with a language model stapled on top.

The dirty secret of the agent hype cycle: nobody wants to talk about the boring stuff. Error recovery? Not sexy. Session persistence? No Twitter engagement. Graceful degradation when your API provider decides 2am is maintenance time? Forget it.

But that's the entire game.

Your agent demo looks incredible. It chains five tools together and produces a beautiful output. Then it hits production and silently fails on run 47 because a JSON response had a null where it expected a string. Nobody notices for three days.

I've been running agents autonomously for months. The painful truth: I spend maybe 15% of my time on prompts and model logic. The other 85% is logging, retry logic, state management, and building the kind of boring infrastructure that makes the agent actually reliable.

Stop chasing model upgrades. Start building plumbing.

posts.title: Clay scoring pipeline from scratch
posts.hook:
Built a lead scoring pipeline last week that replaced 3 hours of manual research per account.

Total cost per run: $0.12.
posts.body:
Built a lead scoring pipeline last week that replaced 3 hours of manual research per account.

Total cost per run: $0.12.

The stack:

🔧 Clay table pulls new accounts from Attio CRM via webhook
🔧 Enrichment column hits Apollo for org data, job postings, tech stack
🔧 AI column scores fit against ICP criteria (headcount, funding stage, tech signals)
🔧 Second AI column writes a 2-sentence research brief per account
🔧 Score + brief push back to Attio via HTTP column

The scoring prompt is the whole game. Don't ask the model "is this a good lead." Give it your actual ICP definition, weight the signals, and force a 1-10 score with reasoning.

Mine weights hiring velocity 3x because companies actively hiring for the role I sell into convert at 4x the rate of cold accounts.

The brief column is what makes reps actually use it. Nobody reads a score. Everyone reads "Series B fintech, just posted 3 SDR roles, using Outreach but no enrichment tool."

Total build time was about 90 minutes. Most of that was tuning the scoring prompt against 50 known-good accounts to calibrate.
posts.cta: If you're still scoring leads manually or using static filters, the ROI on this kind of build is immediate.

remixes.label: before-and-after
remixes.hook:
Last month our lead scoring process: open LinkedIn, read the company page, check Crunchbase, skim their careers page, make a gut call.

Time per account: 45 minutes.
Accuracy: whatever I felt like that day.
remixes.body:
Last month our lead scoring process: open LinkedIn, read the company page, check Crunchbase, skim their careers page, make a gut call.

Time per account: 45 minutes.
Accuracy: whatever I felt like that day.

Now it's a Clay pipeline that runs in under 90 seconds.

A webhook fires when a new account lands in Attio. Clay pulls the company record, enriches it through Apollo for org data, headcount, tech stack, and open roles. An AI column scores fit against specific ICP criteria. Not vibes. Weighted signals: engineering headcount above a threshold, specific technologies in the stack, funding stage, hiring velocity.

A second AI column writes a two-sentence research brief explaining why the score is what it is. That brief goes straight into the CRM record so when I open an account, I don't just see a number. I see reasoning.

The whole thing costs about twelve cents per run.

The biggest shift wasn't speed, though. It was consistency. The pipeline scores every account the same way every time. No Monday-brain. No skipping research because I'm tired. No unconscious bias toward companies with cool logos.

If your scoring is still a human reading websites and making calls, you're leaving both time and accuracy on the table.
remixes.cta: What signals do you weigh heaviest when scoring accounts? I'm always tuning the criteria and curious what others prioritize.

remixes.label: cost-breakdown
remixes.hook:
Lead scoring cost breakdown:

Before: $150/hour (my time) x 3 hours = $450 per batch.
After: $0.12 per batch. Same output. Better consistency.
remixes.body:
Lead scoring cost breakdown:

Before: $150/hour (my time) x 3 hours = $450 per batch.
After: $0.12 per batch. Same output. Better consistency.

Here's what twelve cents buys you:

Clay webhook catches new accounts from Attio CRM. No manual trigger needed. Enrichment columns pull org data from Apollo: headcount, funding, tech stack, open roles. An AI scoring column weighs those signals against ICP criteria you define once and never think about again.

But the part that actually matters is the second AI column. It writes a research brief. Two sentences explaining why this account scored the way it did. That brief lives in the CRM record.

So when I open an account, I don't see a mystery number. I see: "Series B SaaS, 120 engineers, hiring for platform roles, running Kubernetes and Terraform. Strong fit for infrastructure tooling."

That context used to take me 20 minutes per account to produce manually. Now it's generated in seconds and it's more thorough than what I was writing by hand.

The ROI math is almost embarrassing. Even if you only score 50 accounts a month, you're saving 150 hours annually. At any reasonable hourly rate, that's a significant number.

The tooling exists. The APIs are cheap. The only question is whether you're going to keep doing it manually.
remixes.cta: If you want the Clay template for this exact pipeline, drop a comment. Happy to share the setup.

Total cost per run: $0.12.

Here's the architecture I recommend if you're building something similar.

Start with the data trigger. A Clay table connected to your CRM via webhook ensures every new account enters the pipeline automatically. No manual intervention, no accounts slipping through.

For enrichment, route through Apollo for organizational data: headcount, funding stage, tech stack, and open positions. These signals form the foundation of any credible scoring model.

The scoring layer should be an AI column with explicit, weighted ICP criteria. Not a vague prompt asking "is this a good lead." Define the signals that matter: engineering team size, specific technologies, growth velocity, market segment. Make the model show its reasoning.

Add a second AI column that generates a two-sentence research brief per account. This is the most underrated step. Scores without context are just numbers. Your sales team needs to understand why an account scored high to act on it effectively.

The pipeline pushes scored accounts back to your CRM with the brief attached. Total latency is under two minutes per batch.

I recommend starting with 5 ICP signals and iterating from there. Overcomplicating the scoring criteria upfront is the most common mistake I see.

posts.title: Daily loop of a solo GTM builder
posts.hook:
No co-founder. No team. No VC.

Just a Mac Mini, a Claude subscription, and crons that run while I sleep.
posts.body:
No co-founder. No team. No VC.

Just a Mac Mini, a Claude subscription, and crons that run while I sleep.

The daily loop looks like this:

Morning: check what the overnight crons produced. Blog post drafted, SEO keywords rotated, enrichment data refreshed. Review, approve, publish.

Mid-day: build the next piece of the system. Today it's a new enrichment pipeline. Tomorrow it's fixing why the agent hallucinated a company name at 2am.

Afternoon: client delivery. The systems I build for myself are the same ones I sell.

Evening: ship whatever I built. Push to prod. Write the context handoff so tomorrow's session picks up clean.

The thing Pieter Levels gets right that most people miss. It's not about having great ideas. It's about having a production loop that compounds. Every system I build makes the next one faster.

My blog generator writes 5 posts a week autonomously. Not because the AI is magic, but because I spent 3 weeks building the voice system, the keyword rotation, the editorial pipeline around it.

The boring infrastructure is the product. The AI is just the engine inside it.
posts.cta: Solo builders shipping real systems. That's the most underrated category in tech right now.

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo founder building and shipping multiple products like Nomad List without VC funding. He emphasizes rapid iteration, transparency in revenue figures, and the power of building in public to attract users organically. His approach has led to sustainable businesses generating six figures monthly.

remixes.label: machine-as-cofounder
remixes.hook: My co-founder is a Mac Mini that never sleeps, never complains, and ships code at midnight.
remixes.body:
My co-founder is a Mac Mini that never sleeps, never complains, and ships code at midnight.

I'm not being cute. This is literally how I operate.

While I sleep, cron jobs run on a single machine sitting on my desk. A blog post gets drafted from trending topics in my niche. SEO keywords rotate based on what's performing. Enrichment data refreshes across the CRM. Lead scoring pipelines process new accounts.

I wake up to a queue of finished work. My job in the morning is editorial, not operational. Review what the machines produced. Approve, tweak, or reject. Publish what's ready.

By 9am I've shipped more than most teams ship by lunch. Not because I'm faster. Because the work started 8 hours before I woke up.

The rest of my day is building the next system. Today it's a new enrichment pipeline. Tomorrow it's fixing a parsing bug in the blog generator. Next week it's adding a new data source to the scoring model.

Every system I build makes the overnight queue longer. Every overnight run gives me more leverage the next morning.

No employees to manage. No standups. No sprint ceremonies. Just systems that compound.

People ask how I compete with funded teams. I don't compete on headcount. I compete on leverage per hour. And the gap widens every week because the systems keep stacking.
remixes.cta: What's the first thing you'd automate if you had an always-on machine running jobs overnight?

remixes.label: anti-hustle-framing
remixes.hook:
The least "hustle" way to build a business:

Automate first. Do the remaining work manually. Repeat.
remixes.body:
The least "hustle" way to build a business:

Automate first. Do the remaining work manually. Repeat.

I don't grind 14-hour days. I build systems that run while I'm not working, then I spend my actual work hours making those systems better.

Here's what a real day looks like.

I open my laptop around 8am. Overnight crons have already run: blog content drafted, SEO keywords rotated, enrichment data pulled, leads scored. My first hour is reviewing output and publishing what's ready. That's editorial work, not grunt work.

Mid-day I pick one system to improve. Not three. Not five. One. Maybe the blog generator is producing intros that need too much editing. So I tune the prompt, adjust the pipeline, run a test batch. Ship it.

Afternoon I do the things that actually require a human. Client calls. Strategy decisions. Writing that needs my actual voice, not a generated draft.

By 5pm I'm done. The machines pick up the overnight shift.

This isn't a flex. It's a design choice. Solo doesn't mean doing everything yourself. It means building the infrastructure so you only do the things that require you.

The compounding effect is real. Every system I build reduces tomorrow's manual work. Six months of this and the leverage is absurd.
remixes.cta: Solo builders who design for leverage instead of hustle. That's the category I'm betting on.

The key principle: every system you build should reduce tomorrow's manual work. Blog generation, SEO rotation, lead enrichment, data scoring. Each one you automate adds to the overnight queue and subtracts from your morning workload.

posts.title: Agent failures are infra failures
posts.hook: Hot take: 90% of AI agent failures have nothing to do with the model.
posts.body:
Hot take: 90% of AI agent failures have nothing to do with the model.

They're infrastructure failures wearing an AI mask.

Your agent didn't "hallucinate." Your context window overflowed because you didn't chunk the input. Your agent didn't "get confused." The API it called returned a 429 and you had no retry logic. Your agent didn't "fail to reason." You gave it 47 tools with overlapping descriptions and no routing layer.

Karpathy keeps saying this. The model is the easy part. The hard part is everything around it.

I run Claude as an autonomous agent across multiple pipelines. The model almost never surprises me. But the infrastructure surprises me constantly:

... a launchd service silently dying because the plist had the wrong path
... a webhook failing because Cloudflare cached the response
... a database not updating because the dev server stopped and nobody noticed for 11 days

None of those are AI problems. They're ops problems. The same ops problems that have existed for decades.

The teams winning with agents right now aren't AI researchers. They're the ones who know how to run reliable systems and happen to put a model inside one.
posts.cta: Invest in your agent's infrastructure before you upgrade the model. The returns are 10x better.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores the infrastructure challenges in scaling AI agents for real-world automation tasks. Details how custom runtimes and vector databases are becoming essential for agent memory and tool integration. Predicts a shift towards agent-native developer workflows in the coming months.

remixes.hook:
Stop blaming the AI model.

Your agent didn't fail because GPT-4 is dumb. It failed because your error handling is nonexistent.
remixes.body:
Stop blaming the AI model.

Your agent didn't fail because GPT-4 is dumb. It failed because your error handling is nonexistent.

I see this pattern constantly. Team builds an agent. Agent fails in production. Team's response: "We need a better model." They upgrade from GPT-4 to Claude. Agent still fails. They add more few-shot examples. Still fails. They rewrite the system prompt seventeen times.

Meanwhile, the actual bug is a missing retry on an HTTP call.

Here's a real example from my own systems. I had an agent that "randomly stopped working" every few days. Spent two hours tuning the prompt. Then I checked the logs properly and found it: the enrichment API was returning 429s during peak hours. The agent received an error response, tried to parse it as valid data, and silently produced garbage output.

The fix wasn't a better prompt. It was three lines of retry logic with exponential backoff.

Another one: agent "hallucinating" wrong company data. Actual cause: the context window was overflowing because I was stuffing the entire company profile instead of chunking it. The model was doing its best with truncated input.

The pattern is always the same. What looks like an AI failure is almost always an infrastructure failure. Bad input handling, missing error recovery, no validation on tool outputs, inadequate logging.

Debug your plumbing before you blame the model.
remixes.cta: Next time your agent "fails," check the infrastructure first. I'd bet money the model isn't the problem.

remixes.label: agent-failure-taxonomy
remixes.hook:
A taxonomy of AI agent failures.

Spoiler: only one category is actually the model's fault.
remixes.body:
A taxonomy of AI agent failures.

Spoiler: only one category is actually the model's fault.

Category 1: Input failures.
Context window overflow, malformed data from upstream APIs, missing fields that the agent expects. The model never had a chance because it received garbage input. Fix: validate and chunk everything before it touches the model.

Category 2: Tool failures.
APIs returning errors, rate limits, timeouts, unexpected response schemas. The agent calls a tool and gets back something it can't parse. Fix: retry logic, response validation, fallback paths.

Category 3: State failures.
Agent loses track of where it is in a multi-step process. Session dies mid-run and there's no recovery. Partial writes to a database with no rollback. Fix: persistent state, checkpointing, idempotent operations.

Category 4: Observability failures.
The agent did something wrong and nobody knows why. No decision logs, no trace of which tool returned what, no way to replay the failure. Fix: structured logging of every decision point and tool call.

Category 5: Actual model failures.
The model genuinely reasoned incorrectly given clean input and working tools. This happens. But it's maybe 10% of production agent failures in my experience.

When you categorize your agent failures this way, the investment priorities become obvious. Categories 1 through 4 are all standard software engineering. Fix those first.
remixes.cta: Which category bites you most often? For me it's Category 2. Third-party APIs are the bane of production agents.

tones.builder:
Hot take: 90% of AI agent failures have nothing to do with the model.

They're infrastructure failures wearing an AI mask.

Your agent didn't "hallucinate." Your context window overflowed because you didn't chunk the input. Your agent didn't "get confused." The API it called returned a 429 and you had no retry logic. Your agent didn't "fail to reason." You gave it 47 tools with overlapping descriptions and no routing layer.

Karpathy keeps saying this. The model is the easy part. The hard part is everything around it.

I run Claude as an autonomous agent across multiple pipelines. The model almost never surprises me. But the infrastructure surprises me constantly:

... a launchd service silently dying because the plist had the wrong path
... a webhook failing because Cloudflare cached the response
... a database not updating because the dev server stopped and nobody noticed for 11 days

None of those are AI problems. They're ops problems. The same ops problems that have existed for decades.

The teams winning with agents right now aren't AI researchers. They're the ones who know how to run reliable systems and happen to put a model inside one.
tones.advisor:
Hot take: 90% of AI agent failures have nothing to do with the model.

They're infrastructure failures wearing an AI mask.

Here's how I recommend diagnosing agent issues before reaching for a model upgrade.

First, audit your input pipeline. When an agent "hallucinates," check whether the context window overflowed or the input data was malformed. Most hallucination reports I investigate trace back to truncated or corrupted context. Chunk your inputs and validate them before they reach the model.

Second, examine your tool integration layer. When an agent "gets confused," check whether an API returned a 429 or an unexpected schema. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff and validate every tool response before passing it back to the model.

Third, review your tool descriptions. When an agent "fails to reason," check whether you gave it 47 tools with overlapping descriptions. The model can't choose correctly when the options are ambiguous. Consolidate overlapping tools and write precise, distinct descriptions.

Fourth, invest in observability. You need to trace every decision the agent makes: which tool it called, what it received, why it chose path B over path A. Without this, you're debugging blind.

In my experience running production agents, fixing these four areas resolves roughly 90% of failures. The remaining 10% might warrant a model change. But start with the infrastructure.

And the agent framework vendors love it that way. Because if you think the problem is the model, you'll upgrade to their premium tier. If you think the problem is the framework, you'll migrate to their newer SDK. If you think the problem is the prompt, you'll buy their prompt engineering course.

Nobody's selling "add three lines of retry logic" as a solution. But that's the actual fix most of the time.

Your agent didn't hallucinate. Your context window overflowed because you crammed 50,000 tokens into a 32K window and hoped for the best. Your agent didn't get confused. The API it called returned an error and you had zero handling for non-200 responses. Your agent didn't fail to reason. You gave it a toolbox where four tools do essentially the same thing with slightly different names.

These aren't AI problems. These are software engineering problems that have existed for decades. We have solutions. They're called input validation, error handling, retry logic, and clean API design.

The most expensive debugging session is the one where you spend a week prompt-engineering around a bug that three lines of infrastructure code would have prevented.

Stop upgrading the model. Fix the plumbing.

posts.title: 3-layer personalization that works
posts.hook:
Personalized outbound that doesn't sound like a robot wrote it.

It's a 3-layer problem, not a prompt problem.
posts.body:
Personalized outbound that doesn't sound like a robot wrote it.

It's a 3-layer problem, not a prompt problem.

Layer 1: Account context
Company size, funding, tech stack, recent news. This is the easy part. Apollo and Clay handle it. But this alone gives you "I saw you raised a Series B, congrats!" energy. Table stakes.

Layer 2: Role context
What does this person's job actually look like day to day? An SDR manager at a 200-person SaaS company has different pain than a CRO at a 2000-person enterprise. Map the role to specific workflow problems, not generic value props.

Layer 3: Timing context
Why now? Job postings, leadership changes, tech stack shifts, competitor moves. This is where AI enrichment actually earns its cost. A Clay column that checks for recent SDR job postings tells you they're scaling outbound right now.

The sequence then writes itself. You're not asking AI to be creative. You're giving it three layers of real context and asking it to connect the dots.

I used to do this manually as an SDR. 200+ emails a day from primary domains. No warmup. The research per account took 15-20 minutes. Now the enrichment pipeline does it in seconds, and the quality is higher because it checks more sources than I ever could.
posts.cta: Layer 2 is where most teams skip. Role-level research is the difference between "personalized" and actually personal.

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down effective outbound automation without sounding spammy. Advises on personalized sequences using AI tools while maintaining human touch. Provides real examples of sequences that boosted reply rates by 30%.

remixes.label: why-outbound-feels-fake
remixes.hook:
"I saw you raised a Series B, congrats!"

Every founder has gotten this email 400 times. It's not personalization. It's a template with a variable.
remixes.body:
"I saw you raised a Series B, congrats!"

Every founder has gotten this email 400 times. It's not personalization. It's a template with a variable.

The problem with most automated outbound is that it stops at the company layer. Funding round, headcount, tech stack. Apollo and Clay make this trivially easy. Which means everyone does it. Which means it's noise.

The layer that actually gets responses is role context. Not what the company does, but what this specific person's job looks like day to day.

A VP of Engineering at a 200-person startup doesn't care that you know they raised money. They care about the three things keeping them up at night: hiring velocity, deployment reliability, and whether their platform team can ship the internal tooling roadmap before Q3.

When your outbound speaks to those specifics, it doesn't read like outbound anymore. It reads like someone who understands their world.

But there's a third layer most people miss entirely: timing context. What's happening at this company right now that makes your message relevant this week, not just this quarter? A new job posting for a role your product replaces. A blog post about a problem you solve. A conference talk about a challenge you address.

Three layers: company, role, timing. Most outbound nails one, attempts two, and ignores three.
remixes.cta: Which layer does your outbound stop at? Be honest. Company-level personalization is table stakes now.

remixes.label: build-the-stack
remixes.hook:
The technical stack behind outbound that doesn't sound automated.

It's three enrichment layers, not one big prompt.
remixes.body:
The technical stack behind outbound that doesn't sound automated.

It's three enrichment layers, not one big prompt.

Layer 1 is account enrichment. This is solved. Apollo gives you company size, funding, tech stack, recent news. Clay orchestrates it. You pipe it into your CRM. If you're not doing this already, start here. But know that this is baseline. Everyone has access to this data.

Layer 2 is role enrichment. This is where it gets interesting. What does this person's specific job look like? Not their title. Their actual responsibilities and pain points. You get this from job postings for similar roles at their company, their LinkedIn activity, conference talks, blog posts they've written. Clay can pull some of this. Some of it takes custom scraping.

Layer 3 is timing enrichment. Why should they care right now? New job postings signal team growth or gaps. Recent product launches signal priorities. Earnings calls signal budget shifts. This layer turns "relevant message" into "relevant message this week."

The prompt that writes the actual email gets all three layers as context. It's not doing the personalization. It's assembling pre-researched context into natural language. That's a much easier job for an AI model than trying to generate insight from scratch.

Stack the enrichment right and even a basic model writes emails that feel hand-researched.
remixes.cta: Layer 2 is the hardest to automate well. What data sources do you use for role-level research?

posts.title: Practitioners should write revenue content
posts.hook:
Revenue content written by marketers sounds like marketing.

Revenue content written by operators sounds like money.
posts.body:
Revenue content written by marketers sounds like marketing.

Revenue content written by operators sounds like money.

I keep seeing demand gen teams produce content like "5 Ways to Improve Your Pipeline" with generic advice that any SDR could debunk in 30 seconds.

Meanwhile, the posts that actually drive inbound are practitioners sharing exactly what they built. Column names in Clay. Scoring logic in their CRM. The prompt that took their enrichment from 60% to 94% accuracy.

The difference is specificity.

Marketing content says "leverage AI for personalization." Practitioner content says "I added a Claygent column that checks the prospect's company blog for recent product launches, then feeds that into the email intro line. Reply rates went from 3% to 11%."

One sounds smart. The other is actually useful.

This is why the builder-creator hybrid is winning on LinkedIn right now. Not because they're better writers. Because they have real things to write about.

You don't need a content calendar. You need a build log. Ship something, document what worked, share the specifics. That's the whole playbook.

The best demand gen isn't content. It's documentation of real work that happens to generate demand.
posts.cta: If your content team isn't building anything, they're guessing. And your audience can tell.

inspired_by.author: Lara Acosta
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for revenue teams, focusing on demand gen through LinkedIn and newsletters. Stresses repurposing sales enablement content into multi-channel assets. Includes metrics on how content drove 40% pipeline increase.

remixes.label: show-your-work
remixes.hook:
The best performing post I ever wrote took 4 minutes.

It was a screenshot of a Clay table with a caption explaining what each column did.
remixes.body:
The best performing post I ever wrote took 4 minutes.

It was a screenshot of a Clay table with a caption explaining what each column did.

No storytelling framework. No hook formula. Just a practitioner showing their work.

It outperformed every polished thought leadership piece I spent hours drafting. And it wasn't close.

Here's what I've learned about content that actually drives pipeline:

People don't trust advice from people who don't build. They trust screenshots of real dashboards. They trust config files with actual column names. They trust someone saying "I tried X, it broke, here's what I changed."

The content teams producing generic "5 Tips" posts are competing with thousands of identical articles. The operator who posts a 2-minute Loom walking through their scoring logic is competing with almost nobody.

This is why practitioners are becoming the new content engine at smart companies. Not because they're better writers. Because they have receipts.

Your marketing team can interview them, edit their rough drafts, turn their Slack messages into posts. But someone has to have actually built the thing first.
remixes.cta: Receipts beat rhetoric. Show the build, not the theory.

remixes.label: marketer-vs-builder-gap
remixes.hook:
Your demand gen team is writing about a product they've never configured.

Your prospects can smell it immediately.
remixes.body:
Your demand gen team is writing about a product they've never configured.

Your prospects can smell it immediately.

I ran an experiment last quarter. Published two posts on the same topic, same day.

Post A: Written by a content marketer after a 30-minute interview with a solutions engineer. Clean structure, proper CTA, on-brand.

Post B: Written by the solutions engineer directly. Rough around the edges. Included a screenshot of a real workflow with field names visible.

Post B got 4x the engagement and generated 3 inbound conversations. Post A got likes from other marketers.

The gap isn't about writing quality. It's about signal density. When someone has actually built the thing, every sentence carries weight. When someone is summarizing what they heard in a meeting, you get filler dressed up as insight.

The fix isn't firing your content team. It's restructuring who creates the raw material. Engineers, RevOps people, customer success managers. They have the stories. Your content team should be editing and distributing, not originating.

The companies winning at content right now have operators who post. Period.
remixes.cta: Put your builders in front of the audience. Let marketing amplify, not originate.

posts.title: Agent frameworks after actually shipping
posts.hook:
Used LangGraph, CrewAI, and raw Claude API calls in production.

After 6 months, here's where each one actually fits.
posts.body:
Used LangGraph, CrewAI, and raw Claude API calls in production.

After 6 months, here's where each one actually fits.

Raw API calls win for single-purpose agents. My blog generator, enrichment pipelines, scoring systems. They all use direct Claude API. No framework overhead. When the agent does one thing well, a framework just adds abstraction you don't need.

CrewAI works for multi-agent coordination where agents have distinct roles. I tested it for a pipeline where one agent researches, one scores, one writes. It handles the orchestration. But debugging gets painful fast because the abstraction hides what's actually happening between agents.

LangGraph fits when you need explicit state machines. Complex branching logic where the agent's next step depends on structured evaluation of the previous step. It's more work upfront but the control flow is visible and debuggable.

The pattern I keep seeing: teams pick a framework first, then try to fit their problem into it. Flip that. Define the agent's job, map the control flow on paper, then pick the lightest tool that supports it.

Most production agents I've built ended up being raw API calls with good error handling and a simple state file. The framework would have added complexity without adding capability.
posts.cta: Start with the simplest thing that works. Add framework when you feel the pain, not before.

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Reviews the latest in open-source AI agent toolkits, comparing CrewAI, Semantic Kernel, and LlamaIndex for building automation pipelines. Points out gaps in current developer tools for multi-agent collaboration and suggests infrastructure improvements needed for enterprise adoption.

remixes.label: decision-tree
remixes.hook:
Stop asking "which AI agent framework should I use?"

The answer is almost always: don't use one yet.
remixes.body:
Stop asking "which AI agent framework should I use?"

The answer is almost always: don't use one yet.

I've shipped agents with LangGraph, CrewAI, and raw API calls. Here's the decision tree I actually use now.

Question 1: Does your agent do one thing? Use raw API calls. My blog generator, enrichment pipelines, scoring systems. All direct Claude API. A framework adds abstraction you'll fight against more than you'll benefit from.

Question 2: Do multiple agents need to coordinate? Now CrewAI starts making sense. Role assignment, handoffs, shared context. But only when the coordination logic would be painful to write yourself.

Question 3: Do you need complex branching, retries, or stateful workflows? LangGraph. It's the heaviest option but it handles DAG-style orchestration that would be a mess to build from scratch.

The mistake I see constantly: people start with a framework because it looks impressive, then spend weeks fighting the abstractions instead of solving the actual problem.

My enrichment pipeline started as 40 lines of Python hitting the Claude API. Six months later, it's still 40 lines of Python hitting the Claude API. It works. It's fast to debug. No dependency updates breaking things at 2am.

Frameworks are for when the pain of not having one exceeds the pain of learning one. Most people never hit that threshold.
remixes.cta: Pick the lightest tool that solves your problem. Upgrade when the pain is real.

remixes.label: production-scars
remixes.hook:
Every AI agent framework tutorial shows the happy path.

None of them show what happens at 3am when your state graph throws a cryptic error.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent framework tutorial shows the happy path.

None of them show what happens at 3am when your state graph throws a cryptic error.

I've been running agents in production for 6 months across LangGraph, CrewAI, and raw Claude API calls. Here are the scars.

Raw API calls: My blog generator runs nightly. Direct Claude API. When it breaks, the error is obvious. Bad prompt, rate limit, malformed JSON. Fix it in minutes. Downside: building retry logic and error handling from scratch.

CrewAI: Deployed a multi-agent research pipeline. The role abstraction is clean when it works. When it doesn't, debugging which agent dropped context mid-conversation is painful. The framework's error messages assume you understand its internal state machine.

LangGraph: Built a complex scoring workflow with branching logic. Powerful for DAG orchestration. But the learning curve is steep and when a node fails mid-graph, tracing the issue through state transitions takes real effort.

The pattern: complexity of the framework correlates directly with difficulty of debugging in production. Every layer of abstraction you add is a layer you'll eventually need to debug blind at an inconvenient hour.

My rule now: start with raw API calls. Move to a framework only when you can name the specific pain the framework solves.
remixes.cta: The best framework is the one you can debug at 3am without reading source code.

posts.title: RevOps split into two camps
posts.hook: RevOps is splitting into two roles and most orgs haven't noticed yet.
posts.body:
RevOps is splitting into two roles and most orgs haven't noticed yet.

Camp 1: RevOps Analysts. Dashboards, reporting, Salesforce admin, data hygiene. Important work. Keeps the lights on.

Camp 2: RevOps Engineers. Building pipelines in code, writing Clay automations, deploying enrichment systems, connecting APIs that don't have native integrations. Creating leverage.

The gap between these two is growing fast.

Camp 1 configures tools. Camp 2 builds systems that make tools unnecessary.

I lived this transition. Started as an SDR doing manual data entry in Salesforce. Built my first automation because I was tired of copy-pasting between tabs. That automation became a system. That system became a product.

Now every ops stack I build has code in it. Python scripts for enrichment. Clay tables for scoring. Webhook pipelines that move data between tools the vendors never designed to talk to each other.

The RevOps people learning to code aren't replacing the analysts. They're operating at a different altitude. One maintains the CRM. The other builds the intelligence layer on top of it.

Both are needed. But the engineers are getting promoted faster, paid more, and creating disproportionate impact. Because building a system once beats configuring a workflow every quarter.
posts.cta: If you're in RevOps and not learning to code, you're leaving leverage on the table.

inspired_by.author: Dan Tyre
inspired_by.summary: Explores revenue operations as the backbone of scalable sales orgs. Details ops stack for outbound at scale, including automation tools and KPIs. Shares case study of reducing CAC by 25% via revops overhaul.

remixes.label: job-posting-proof
remixes.hook:
I read 50 RevOps job postings last week.

Half of them were actually hiring engineers.
remixes.body:
I read 50 RevOps job postings last week.

Half of them were actually hiring engineers.

The postings said "RevOps Manager" or "Revenue Operations Lead." But the requirements told a different story.

Required: Python or SQL proficiency. Experience with REST APIs. Building custom integrations. Data pipeline management. Familiarity with AI/ML tools.

That's not an ops role. That's an engineering role wearing an ops title.

The other half were traditional RevOps. Salesforce admin, reporting, dashboard creation, data hygiene. Important work, but fundamentally different from building automated enrichment systems in code.

The market is telling us something. The RevOps title is being stretched to cover two completely different skill sets, and it's confusing everyone. Candidates apply for roles they're not qualified for. Hiring managers struggle to evaluate across two different competency models. Teams end up with mismatched expectations.

Some companies are already splitting this explicitly. "RevOps Analyst" handles reporting and CRM admin. "RevOps Engineer" or "GTM Engineer" builds pipelines, writes automations, connects systems through code.

The split is happening whether orgs name it or not. The question is whether your team acknowledges it and hires accordingly, or keeps mashing two different roles into one title and wondering why the job req sits open for 6 months.
remixes.cta: Check your own RevOps job postings. Are you hiring an analyst or an engineer? Name it correctly.

remixes.label: career-fork
remixes.hook:
If you're in RevOps right now, you're standing at a fork.

One path keeps you employed. The other makes you hard to replace.
remixes.body:
If you're in RevOps right now, you're standing at a fork.

One path keeps you employed. The other makes you hard to replace.

Path 1 is the analyst path. Dashboards, Salesforce admin, data hygiene, reporting. Companies will always need this. You'll always have a job. But the ceiling is visible, and AI tools are getting better at this work every quarter.

Path 2 is the engineering path. Building pipelines in code. Writing Clay automations that replace manual processes. Deploying enrichment systems that run while you sleep. Connecting APIs that don't have native integrations.

Path 2 creates leverage. One person building automated systems does the work of a team doing it manually. That leverage shows up in your comp, your title, and your options.

I'm not saying analysts aren't valuable. Clean data and good reporting are the foundation. But the ceiling for someone who can only report on data vs. someone who can build systems that generate and transform data. That gap is widening fast.

The good news: you don't need a CS degree to make the shift. Start with SQL if you don't know it. Then Python basics. Then build one automation that replaces a manual process on your team. That's your proof of concept.

The RevOps people who learn to code in the next 12 months will define the next version of the role. Everyone else will be competing for the same analyst positions.
remixes.cta: Pick the path. The fork is here whether you choose or not.

The RevOps function is diverging into two distinct disciplines, and organizations that recognize this early will hire better and retain longer.

The split is real. Pretending it's not doesn't help anyone.

posts.title: GTM engineer role prediction
posts.hook:
GTM Engineer will be a standard job title at B2B SaaS companies within 18 months.

Right now most of them don't know they need one.
posts.body:
GTM Engineer will be a standard job title at B2B SaaS companies within 18 months.

Right now most of them don't know they need one.

The signal is everywhere. Companies are hiring "RevOps" roles but the job description reads like a software engineer. They want Python, API integrations, data pipelines, AI/ML experience. That's not RevOps. That's GTM engineering.

The role exists because the GTM stack got too complex for no-code configs. You can't build a real enrichment pipeline in Zapier. You can't deploy a scoring model in a spreadsheet. You can't orchestrate multi-source research with drag-and-drop.

So companies are quietly hiring engineers and putting them in revenue teams. Or they're finding SDRs who taught themselves to code (raises hand) and discovering those people build better systems than pure engineers because they understand the workflow they're automating.

SDRs make the best GTM engineers because they've felt every manual step they're now automating. They don't build elegant abstractions that miss the point. They build exactly what eliminates the pain they remember.

The career path is real. SDR --> RevOps --> GTM Engineer. Each step adds technical depth on top of operational intuition.

The companies that figure this out first will have a compounding advantage. Everyone else will keep hiring separately for ops people who can't code and engineers who don't understand GTM.
posts.cta: The role is real. The title is catching up.

inspired_by.author: Aakash Gupta
inspired_by.summary: Defines GTM engineering as engineering-led motion for B2B SaaS. Covers building custom outbound systems with code and no-code tools. Predicts it will dominate sales in 2025 with data on early adopters.

remixes.label: already-exists
remixes.hook:
The GTM Engineer role already exists at hundreds of companies.

They just call it something else.
remixes.body:
The GTM Engineer role already exists at hundreds of companies.

They just call it something else.

I've talked to people with these titles in the past month: RevOps Manager, Marketing Operations Lead, Growth Engineer, Sales Systems Architect, Demand Gen Technologist.

Every single one described the same job. Building data pipelines. Writing API integrations. Deploying AI-powered enrichment and scoring. Automating outbound workflows in code.

The title doesn't exist in most org charts, but the work does. And it's being done by people who learned Python because their CRM couldn't do what they needed. Who started writing Clay automations because manual enrichment was killing their team. Who built their own integrations because the native connectors didn't exist.

Here's what's changing: companies are starting to hire for this explicitly. Job postings are appearing with "GTM Engineer" in the title. Comp is landing between senior RevOps and staff engineer levels. The market is recognizing that this skill set, the intersection of GTM strategy and engineering execution, is distinct and valuable.

Within 18 months, I expect "GTM Engineer" to be as standard as "DevOps Engineer" is today. The same trajectory. A role that started as informal work done by adjacent titles, then got formalized because the demand was undeniable.
remixes.cta: If you're already doing this work under a different title, the market is about to catch up to you.

remixes.label: why-now
remixes.hook:
Three shifts happened simultaneously that made GTM Engineering inevitable.

None of them are going to reverse.
remixes.body:
Three shifts happened simultaneously that made GTM Engineering inevitable.

None of them are going to reverse.

Shift 1: The GTM stack became programmable. Clay, Apollo, and similar tools exposed APIs that let you build custom workflows instead of clicking through UIs. Suddenly the ceiling on what one technical person could automate went from "a few Zapier zaps" to "entire pipeline systems."

Shift 2: AI made unstructured data usable. Before LLMs, enrichment meant structured fields from data providers. Now you can score accounts against nuanced ICP criteria, personalize messaging at scale, and extract signal from 10-K filings, job postings, and tech stack changes. But only if you can write the code to orchestrate it.

Shift 3: The talent pool matured. A generation of RevOps people learned Python during COVID. Marketing ops folks started writing SQL instead of building Marketo programs. These people needed a role that matched their actual skill set.

Put these together and you get a new function: someone who sits between GTM strategy and technical execution. Not a marketer who can use tools. Not an engineer who doesn't understand pipeline. A hybrid role that builds automated GTM systems in code.

This isn't a trend. It's a structural change in how B2B companies operate.
remixes.cta: The three forces creating this role are accelerating, not slowing down.

posts.title: Weekend automation disaster
posts.hook:
Tried to automate my entire outbound pipeline in one weekend.

By Sunday night, I had broken 3 integrations and sent 0 emails.
posts.body:
Tried to automate my entire outbound pipeline in one weekend.

By Sunday night, I had broken 3 integrations and sent 0 emails.

The plan: Clay enrichment --> AI scoring --> personalized sequences --> automated send. Full autopilot.

What actually happened:

🔧 Hour 3: Clay webhook to CRM worked. Felt like a genius.
🔧 Hour 6: Scoring prompt kept rating every account 8+/10 because I wrote the ICP criteria too broadly. Had to score against 50 known accounts to calibrate. Rebuilt the prompt twice.
🔧 Hour 10: Sequence personalization was technically correct but felt hollow. The AI connected the right data points but missed the conversational rhythm. Sounded like a smart robot.
🔧 Hour 14: Realized I'd been enriching the wrong field. 200 accounts processed with company description in the job title column.

The lesson wasn't "automation doesn't work." Every piece eventually worked. The lesson was that automation is a sequence of small bets, not one big deploy.

Now I build one layer at a time. Get enrichment right for a week. Then add scoring. Then add personalization. Each layer gets tested against real data before the next one goes on top.

The instinct to automate everything at once is strong. Resist it. Sequential automation with validation beats parallel automation with hope.
posts.cta: Ship one layer. Validate it. Then build the next. The boring way is the fast way.

remixes.label: layer-by-layer
remixes.hook:
I build every automation one layer at a time.

Not because I'm patient. Because I've been burned too many times doing it the other way.
remixes.body:
I build every automation one layer at a time.

Not because I'm patient. Because I've been burned too many times doing it the other way.

The temptation is always the same. You see the full vision. Clay enrichment feeding AI scoring feeding personalized sequences feeding automated sends. The whole pipeline, end to end. You think: I'll just build it all at once. It'll be beautiful.

It won't be beautiful. It'll be 4 broken integrations with no way to tell which one caused the failure.

Here's my rule now: one layer per session. Build the enrichment step. Run it on 50 records. Check the output manually. Fix the edge cases. Then move to scoring.

Why this works: when something breaks in Layer 3, you already know Layers 1 and 2 are solid. The blast radius of any bug is contained to the thing you just built. Debugging goes from "which of these 6 interconnected systems failed?" to "what's wrong with this one step?"

It feels slower. It's dramatically faster. Because the weekend warrior approach of building everything at once always ends the same way. Sunday night, nothing works, no idea where it broke, starting over Monday morning.

I've shipped more automation with this boring, sequential approach than I ever did trying to build the whole machine in one sprint.
remixes.cta: One layer. Validate. Next layer. The boring method ships more than the ambitious one.

remixes.label: failure-anatomy
remixes.hook:
Every automation disaster I've had follows the exact same pattern.

Here's the pattern so you can recognize it before you waste a weekend.
remixes.body:
Every automation disaster I've had follows the exact same pattern.

Here's the pattern so you can recognize it before you waste a weekend.

Step 1: Early win. First integration works in under an hour. You feel brilliant. This is the most dangerous moment. Confidence outpaces competence.

Step 2: Scope creep by excitement. That first win makes you think the whole pipeline will go this smoothly. You skip validation steps. You start building Layer 3 before testing Layer 2.

Step 3: Silent failures. Something breaks, but nothing throws an error. Your scoring model rates every account highly because the ICP criteria were too broad. Your enrichment returns empty fields but the pipeline keeps running. Data flows downstream looking fine, but it's garbage.

Step 4: Cascade. One bad layer poisons everything after it. Your personalized emails reference enrichment fields that never populated. Your sequencing tool sends messages based on scores that were meaningless.

Step 5: The wipe. Sunday night. Nothing works. You can't trace the root cause because 4 things broke at different points. You delete the whole setup and start over Monday.

The fix is brutally simple. Test each layer independently before connecting it to the next. Validate output quality on small batches. Add checks for empty fields and default scores. Treat automation like you'd treat code. Ship increments, not monuments.
remixes.cta: Recognize the pattern. Early win plus unchecked excitement equals Sunday night disaster.

The lesson wasn't "automation doesn't work." Every piece eventually worked. The lesson was that automation is a sequence of small bets, not one big deploy.

Now I build one layer at a time. Get enrichment right for a week. Then add scoring. Then add personalization. Each layer gets tested against real data before the next one goes on top.

The instinct to automate everything at once is strong. Resist it. Sequential automation with validation beats parallel automation with hope.
tones.advisor:
I want to share a common failure mode I see when teams try to automate outbound pipelines, and the approach I recommend instead.

The typical mistake: building the entire pipeline in one pass. Enrichment, scoring, personalization, sequencing, automated sends. All wired together before any single layer has been validated independently.

This fails predictably. Here's what goes wrong.

The enrichment layer returns partial data but doesn't throw errors. The scoring model over-indexes because ICP criteria are too broad. Everything looks like it's working, but the data flowing through the system is low quality. By the time emails go out, the personalization references empty fields and the targeting is wrong.

My recommended approach: build and validate each layer in isolation.

First, set up enrichment. Run it on 50-100 records. Manually inspect the output. Fix edge cases with empty fields and bad data.

Second, build scoring. Test it against accounts you already know are good and bad fits. Tune the criteria until it matches your intuition.

Third, connect enrichment to scoring. Validate the combined output.

Continue this pattern. Each layer gets tested before connecting to the next. When something breaks, you know exactly where it broke.

This approach takes longer upfront but eliminates the Sunday night wipe-and-rebuild cycle that ambitious automation attempts almost always produce.
tones.provocateur:
You spent the whole weekend building an automation pipeline and shipped zero emails. I know because I've done the exact same thing. Probably three times.

The automation community has a dirty secret: most complex pipelines get built and rebuilt 3-4 times before they actually work. All that content about "I automated my entire outbound in a weekend" is survivorship bias. For every success post, ten people quietly deleted their broken setup on Sunday night.

 about multi-step automation.

Your scoring model will be too generous because you wrote the ICP criteria while excited about the project. Every account will score 8+/10. You'll send broad, unfocused outreach to everyone.

Your enrichment will silently fail on 30% of records. No error. Just empty fields that your downstream templates reference, creating emails that say "Hi {first_name}, I noticed {company} is..." Instant credibility killer.

Your webhook will work beautifully in testing and fail in production because the production payload has one extra nested field you didn't account for.

: the boring approach works. One layer at a time. Validate before connecting. Ship incrementally. It feels slow, but it's the only way that actually results in working automation instead of impressive-looking broken architecture.
